ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general shrimp food product class; no specific BBQ/grilled prawn subclass exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJK covers shrimps and prawns as a category; no specific barbecued preparation subcode identified. closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 identifies prawn as a food substance; no preparation-specific code for BBQ prawns. closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of BBQ prawns in Singapore consists of approximately 4 to 6 medium-large whole prawns, weighing around 200 g including shells.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15152); adapted with marinade contribution from USDA Foundation Foods grilled shrimp data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp (NDB 15152 / FDC ID 175180). Sodium and fat values adjusted upward to account for typical Chinese-style BBQ marinade (soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil) consistent with standard recipe proportions. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is a medium-confidence adaptation; exact restaurant formulation is unknown.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate for plain cooked shrimp/seafood protein food
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care — peer-reviewed journal (American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for BBQ prawns. Prawns are a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate food. GI is estimated at 5 by analogy with other cooked seafood and protein-dominant foods reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (5 × 11.6 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.58, rounded to 0.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit BBQ prawn intake or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a 200 g serving provides approximately 1360 mg sodium — more than half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: BBQ prawns have a very low glycemic index (estimated GI 5) and low carbohydrate load, making them a suitable protein-rich option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the sugar in the marinade should be noted for strict carbohydrate counters.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and dietary cholesterol, partially offset by omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While prawns provide beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and lean protein, the high sodium content from the BBQ marinade and the moderate dietary cholesterol (approximately 304 mg per 200 g serving) warrant portion control for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: BBQ Prawns (烧烤虾; Udang Bakar) are whole prawns marinated in a Chinese-style sauce — typically combining soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, sesame oil, sugar, and white pepper — then grilled or barbecued over charcoal or a gas flame until the shells are lightly charred and the flesh is cooked through. In Singapore, this dish is a popular seafood restaurant staple, commonly served at zi char (煮炒) stalls and seafood restaurants such as The Seafood Market. The prawns are typically served whole-in-shell, requiring diners to peel them at the table, and are accompanied by a dipping sauce or chilli. The texture is firm and slightly smoky, with a sweet-savoury glaze from the marinade. Nutritionally, BBQ prawns are a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate food. Per 100 g, they provide approximately 148 kcal, 17.8 g protein, 5.2 g fat, and 5.8 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is elevated due to the soy-based marinade (approximately 680 mg per 100 g), making portion awareness important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI 5), making this dish suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Prawns are also a good source of selenium, phosphorus, vitamin B12, and cholesterol. The dish is halal-friendly when prepared without alcohol-based marinades.
common_user_queries: How many calories in BBQ prawns?
Is BBQ prawn high in sodium?
What is the GI of BBQ prawns?
How much protein in grilled prawns?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry for BBQ prawns was identified. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data with marinade adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base ingredient (cooked shrimp) is well-characterised in USDA data and the marinade adjustment is consistent with standard recipe proportions.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for cooked shrimp. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked shrimp; naturally zero in unprocessed seafood). Sodium value of 680 mg per 100 g reflects an upward adaptation from the base USDA cooked shrimp value (~566 mg) to account for soy sauce and oyster sauce marinade typical of Chinese-style BBQ preparation. GI of 5 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for BBQ prawns was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the access date. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 5 × 11.6 g carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 = 0.58, rounded to 0.6. The cuisine_tag 'Crab' in the input record appears to be a platform tagging artefact; this record is for prawns only. Price data (SGD 28.00) preserved from input for reference but not used in nutritional processing.
